judgments.comparative_verdict: Report 1 covers all four expectations (including the caveat to re-verify live usage numbers before relying on the stale snapshot), while Report 2 mentions the snapshot's timestamp but never states it would re-check current/live limits before acting on it, so Report 1 answers the task more completely.

report_1.evidence: so the run needs roughly 5,200 × 1,800 ≈ 9.36 million tokens. Against the telemetry in .cache/usage_snapshot.json ... northpeak (the default backend): 640,000 tokens remaining of a 10,000,000-token window — only about 7% of what the run needs.

report_2.evidence: That's 5,200 × 1,800 ≈ 9.36 million tokens for the full run. ... northpeak (primary/default) | 10,000,000 | 640,000 | ... The default backend has only ~640k tokens left — about 7% of what the run needs

report_1.evidence: ridgeline (secondary): 10,800,000 tokens remaining of a 12,000,000-token window — comfortably above the ~9.36M requirement... I'd route the whole run to the secondary backend: ... python3 scripts/score_batch.py --dataset data/manifest.json --provider secondary

report_2.evidence: The secondary backend has 10.8M remaining, comfortably above the ~9.36M needed... Instead I'd launch directly against the secondary backend: ... python3 scripts/score_batch.py --dataset data/manifest.json --provider secondary

report_1.evidence: I would not run run_nightly.sh as-is. On the default path the job would exhaust northpeak's remaining 640k tokens after roughly 350 transcripts (640,000 ÷ 1,800) and then hit rate-limit failures for the remaining ~4,850

report_2.evidence: I would not run run_nightly.sh as-is. It would exhaust northpeak's remaining allowance roughly 355 transcripts in and the job would fail or stall mid-run

report_1.evidence: the usage snapshot is ~3 hours stale (generated 19:25Z vs. the current 22:30Z), so before committing ~9.4M tokens I'd refresh the telemetry (or otherwise re-check live remaining capacity) rather than trust the stale numbers
